lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is the cause, it is the cause, my soul—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let me not name it to you, you chaste stars!—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is the cause. Yet I’ll not shed her blood;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nor scar that whiter skin of hers than snow,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And smooth as monumental alabaster.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yet she must die, else she’ll betray more men.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Put out the light, and then put out the light:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If I quench thee, thou flaming minister,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I can again thy former light restore,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Should I repent me: but once put out thy light,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thou cunning’st pattern of excelling nature,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I know not where is that Promethean heat

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That can thy light relume. When I have pluck’d the rose,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I cannot give it vital growth again.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It must needs wither: I’ll smell it on the tree. Kissing her.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ah balmy breath, that dost almost persuade

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Justice to break her sword! One more, one more.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Be thus when thou art dead, and I will kill thee,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And love thee after. One more, and this the last:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: So sweet was ne’er so fatal. I must weep,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But they are cruel tears: this sorrow’s heavenly;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It strikes where it doth love. She wakes.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Who’s there? Othello?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay. Desdemona.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Will you come to bed, my lord?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Have you pray’d to-night, Desdemona?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Ay, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If you bethink yourself of any crime

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Unreconciled as yet to heaven and grace,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Solicit for it straight.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas, my lord, what do you mean by that?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Well, do it, and be brief; I will walk by:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I would not kill thy unprepared spirit;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: No; heaven forfend! I would not kill thy soul.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Talk you of killing?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay, I do.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Then heaven

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Have mercy on me!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Amen, with all my heart!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: If you say so, I hope you will not kill me.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Hum!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And yet I fear you; for you are fatal then

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: When your eyes roll so: why I should fear I know not,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Since guiltiness I know not; but yet I feel I fear.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Think on thy sins.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: They are loves I bear to you.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay, and for that thou diest.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: That death’s unnatural that kills for loving.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas, why gnaw you so your nether lip?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Some bloody passion shakes your very frame:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: These are portents; but yet I hope, I hope,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: They do not point on me.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Peace, and be still!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I will so. What’s the matter?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That handkerchief which I so loved and gave thee

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thou gavest to Cassio.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: No, by my life and soul!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Send for the man, and ask him.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Sweet soul, take heed,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Take heed of perjury; thou art on thy deathbed.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Ay, but not yet to die.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yes, presently:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Therefore confess thee freely of thy sin;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For to deny each article with oath

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Cannot remove nor choke the strong conception

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That I do groan withal. Thou art to die.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Then Lord have mercy on me!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I say, amen.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And have you mercy too! I never did

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Offend you in my life; never loved Cassio

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But with such general warranty of heaven

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: As I might love: I never gave him token.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: By heaven, I saw my handkerchief in’s hand.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O perjured woman! thou dost stone my heart,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And makest me call what I intend to do

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A murder, which I thought a sacrifice:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I saw the handkerchief.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: He found it then;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I never gave it him: send for him hither;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Let him confess a truth.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He hath confess’d.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What, my lord?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That he hath used thee.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How? unlawfully?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: He will not say so.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: No, his mouth is stopp’d;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Honest Iago hath ta’en order for’t.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O! my fear interprets: what, is he dead?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Had all his hairs been lives, my great revenge

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Had stomach for them all.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas! he is betray’d and I undone.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Out, strumpet! weep’st thou for him to my face?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, banish me, my lord, but kill me not!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Down, strumpet!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Kill me to-morrow: let me live to-night!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nay, if you strive—

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But half an hour!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Being done, there is no pause.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But while I say one prayer!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is too late. He stifles her.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My lord, my lord! what, ho! my lord, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What noise is this? Not dead? not yet quite dead?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I that am cruel am yet merciful;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I would not have thee linger in thy pain: So, so.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: What, ho! my lord, my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Who’s there?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, good my lord, I would speak a word with you!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Yes: ’tis Emilia. By and by. She’s dead.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis like she comes to speak of Cassio’s death.—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The noise was here. Ha! no more moving?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Still as the grave. Shall she come in? were’t good?—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I think she stirs again:—no. What’s best to do?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If she come in, she’ll sure speak to my wife:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My wife! my wife! what wife? I have no wife.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O insupportable! O heavy hour!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Methinks it should be now a huge eclipse

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of sun and moon, and that the affrighted globe

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Should yawn at alteration.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I do beseech you

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That I may speak with you, O, good my lord!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I had forgot thee: O, come in, Emilia;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Soft; by and by. Let me the curtains draw.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Where art thou? Unlocks the door.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, my good lord, yonder’s foul murders done!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What, now?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: But now, my lord.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is the very error of the moon;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She comes more nearer earth than she was wont,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And makes men mad.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Cassio, my lord, hath kill’d a young Venetian

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Call’d Roderigo.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Roderigo kill’d!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And Cassio kill’d!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: No, Cassio is not kill’d.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not Cassio kill’d! then murder’s out of tune,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And sweet revenge grows harsh.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, falsely, falsely murder’d!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Alas, what cry is that?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That! what?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Out, and alas! that was my lady’s voice.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Help! help, ho! help! O lady, speak again!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Sweet Desdemona! O sweet mistress, speak!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: A guiltless death I die.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, who hath done this deed?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Nobody; I myself. Farewell

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Commend me to my kind lord: O, farewell!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why, how should she be murder’d?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Alas, who knows?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: You heard her say herself, it was not I.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: She said so: I must needs report the truth.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She’s, like a liar, gone to burning hell:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Twas I that kill’d her.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, the more angel she,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And you the blacker devil!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She turn’d to folly, and she was a whore.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Thou dost belie her, and thou art a devil.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She was false as water.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Thou art rash as fire, to say

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That she was false: O, she was heavenly true!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Cassio did top her; ask thy husband else.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, I were damn’d beneath all depth in hell,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But that I did proceed upon just grounds

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To this extremity. Thy husband knew it all.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My husband!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Thy husband.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That she was false to wedlock?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay, with Cassio. Nay, had she been true,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If heaven would make me such another world

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of one entire and perfect chrysolite,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I’ld not have sold her for it.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My husband!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay, ’twas he that told me first:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: An honest man he is, and hates the slime

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That sticks on filthy deeds.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My husband!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What needs this iteration, woman? I say thy husband.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O mistress, villany hath made mocks with love,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My husband say that she was false!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He, woman;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I say thy husband: dost understand the word?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My friend, thy husband, honest, honest Iago.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: If he say so, may his pernicious soul

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Rot half a grain a day! he lies to the heart:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: She was too fond of her most filthy bargain.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Do thy worst:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: This deed of thine is no more worthy heaven

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Than thou wast worthy her.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Peace, you were best.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Thou hast not half that power to do me harm

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: As I have to be hurt. O gull! O dolt!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: As ignorant as dirt! thou hast done a deed—

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I care not for thy sword; I’ll make thee known,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Though I lost twenty lives.—Help! help, ho! help!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: The Moor hath kill’d my mistress! Murder! murder!

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: What is the matter? How now, general!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O, are you come, Iago? you have done well,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That men must lay their murders on your neck.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: What is the matter?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Disprove this villain, if thou be’st a man:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: He says thou told’st him that his wife was false:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I know thou didst not, thou’rt not such a villain:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Speak, for my heart is full.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I told him what I thought, and told no more

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Than what he found himself was apt and true.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: But did you ever tell him she was false?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I did.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: You told a lie, an odious, damned lie;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Upon my soul, a lie, a wicked lie.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: She false with Cassio!—did you say with Cassio?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: With Cassio, mistress. Go to, charm your tongue.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I will not charm my tongue; I am bound to speak:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: My mistress here lies murder’d in her bed—

lines.speaker: ALL
lines.text: O heavens forfend!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And your reports have set the murder on.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nay, stare not, masters: it is true, indeed.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: ’Tis a strange truth.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: O monstrous act!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Villany, villany, villany!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I think upon’t, I think: I smell’t: O villany!—

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I thought so then:—I’ll kill myself for grief:—

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O villany, villany!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What, are you mad? I charge you, get you home.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Good gentlemen, let me have leave to speak:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: ’Tis proper I obey him, but not now.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Perchance, Iago, I will ne’er go home.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O! O! O! He falls on the bed.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Nay, lay thee down and roar;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: For thou hast kill’d the sweetest innocent

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: That e’er did lift up eye.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, she was foul!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I scarce did know you, uncle: there lies your niece,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Whose breath, indeed, these hands have newly stopp’d:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I know this act shows horrible and grim.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Poor Desdemona! I am glad thy father’s dead:

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Thy match was mortal to him, and pure grief

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Shore his old thread in twain: did he live now,

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: This sight would make him do a desperate turn,

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Yea, curse his better angel from his side,

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: And fall to reprobation.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Tis pitiful; but yet Iago knows

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That she with Cassio hath the act of shame

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A thousand times committed; Cassio confess’d it:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And she did gratify his amorous works

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: With that recognizance and pledge of love

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Which I first gave her; I saw it in his hand:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It was a handkerchief, an antique token

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My father gave my mother.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O heaven! O heavenly powers!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Come, hold your peace.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: ’Twill out, ’twill out: I peace!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: No, I will speak as liberal as the north:

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Let heaven and men and devils, let them all,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: All, all, cry shame against me, yet I’ll speak.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Be wise, and get you home.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I will not. Iago offers to stab Emilia.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Fie!

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Your sword upon a woman?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O thou dull Moor! that handkerchief thou speak’st of

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: I found by fortune and did give my husband;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: For often, with a solemn earnestness,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: More than indeed belong’d to such a trifle,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: He begg’d of me to steal it.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Villainous whore!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: She give it Cassio! no, alas! I found it,

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And I did give’t my husband.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Filth, thou liest!

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: By heaven, I do not, I do not, gentlemen.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: O murderous coxcomb! what should such a fool

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Do with so good a woman?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Are there no stones in heaven

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But what serve for the thunder?—Precious villain! He runs at Iago. Iago, from behind, stabs Emilia, and exit.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: The woman falls; sure, he hath kill’d his wife.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Ay, ay: O, lay me by my mistress’ side.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: He’s gone, but his wife’s kill’d.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: ’Tis a notorious villain. Take you this weapon,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Which I have here recover’d from the Moor:

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Come, guard the door without; let him not pass,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: But kill him rather. I’ll after that same villain,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: For ’tis a damned slave.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I am not valiant neither,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But ever puny whipster gets my sword:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But why should honour outlive honesty?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let it go all.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: What did thy song bode, lady?

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Hark, canst thou hear me? I will play the swan.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: And die in music. Willow, willow, willow—

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: Moor, she was chaste; she loved thee, cruel Moor;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: So come my soul to bliss, as I speak true;

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: So speaking as I think, I die, I die.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have another weapon in this chamber;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is a sword of Spain, the ice-brook’s temper:—

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O, here it is. Uncle, I must come forth.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: If thou attempt it, it will cost thee dear:

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Thou hast no weapon, and perforce must suffer.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Look in upon me then and speak with me,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Or, naked as I am, I will assault thee.

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: What is the matter?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Behold, I have a weapon;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A better never did itself sustain

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Upon a soldier’s thigh: I have seen the day,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That, with this little arm and this good sword,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have made my way through more impediments

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Than twenty times your stop: but, O vain boast!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Who can control his fate? ’tis not so now.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Be not afraid, though you do see me weapon’d;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Here is my journey’s end, here is my butt,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And very sea-mark of my utmost sail.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Do you go back dismay’d? ’tis a lost fear;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Man but a rush against Othello’s breast,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And he retires. Where should Othello go?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Now, how dost thou look now? O ill-starr’d wench!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Pale as thy smock! when we shall meet at compt,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: This look of thine will hurl my soul from heaven,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And fiends will snatch at it. Cold, cold, my girl!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Even like thy chastity. O cursed slave!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Whip me, ye devils,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: From the possession of this heavenly sight!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Blow me about in winds! roast me in sulphur!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Wash me in steep-down gulfs of liquid fire!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O Desdemona! Desdemona! dead!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Oh! Oh! Oh!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Where is this rash and most unfortunate man?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That’s he that was Othello: here I am.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Where is that viper? bring the villain forth.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I look down towards his feet; but that’s a fable.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If that thou be’st a devil, I cannot kill thee. Wounds Iago.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Wrench his sword from him.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I bleed, sir; but not kill’d.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I am not sorry neither: I’ld have thee live;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For, in my sense, ’tis happiness to die.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: O thou Othello, thou wert once so good,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Fall’n in the practise of a damned slave,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: What shall be said to thee?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why, any thing:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: An honourable murderer, if you will;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For nought I did in hate, but all in honour.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: This wretch hath part confess’d his villany:

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Did you and he consent in Cassio’s death?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ay.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Dear general, I never gave you cause.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I do believe it, and I ask your pardon.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Will you, I pray, demand that demi-devil

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Why he hath thus ensnared my soul and body?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Demand me nothing: what you know, you know:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: From this time forth I never will speak word.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: What, not to pray?

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: Torments will ope your lips.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Well, thou dost best.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Sir, you shall understand what hath befall’n,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Which, as I think, you know not. Here is a letter

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Found in the pocket of the slain Roderigo;

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: And here another: the one of them imports

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: The death of Cassio to be undertook

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: By Roderigo.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O villain!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Most heathenish and most gross!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Now here’s another discontented paper,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Found in his pocket too; and this, it seems,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Roderigo meant to have sent this damned villain;

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: But that belike Iago in the interim

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Came in and satisfied him.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O the pernicious caitiff!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How came you, Cassio, by that handkerchief

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That was my wife’s?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I found it in my chamber:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And he himself confess’d but even now

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That there he dropp’d it for a special purpose

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Which wrought to his desire.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O fool! fool! fool!

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: There is besides in Roderigo’s letter,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: How he upbraids Iago, that he made him

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Brave me upon the watch; whereon it came

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That I was cast: and even but now he spake,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: After long seeming dead, Iago hurt him,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Iago set him on.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: You must forsake this room, and go with us:

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Your power and your command is taken off,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: And Cassio rules in Cyprus. For this slave,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: If there be any cunning cruelty

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: That can torment him much and hold him long,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: It shall be his. You shall close prisoner rest,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Till that the nature of your fault be known

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: To the Venetian state. Come, bring him away.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Soft you; a word or two before you go.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have done the state some service, and they know’t.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: No more of that. I pray you, in your letters,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: When you shall these unlucky deeds relate,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Speak of me as I am; nothing extenuate,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nor set down aught in malice: then must you speak

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of one that loved not wisely but too well;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of one not easily jealous, but being wrought

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Perplex’d in the extreme; of one whose hand,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Like the base Indian, threw a pearl away

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Richer than all his tribe; of one whose subdued eyes,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Albeit unused to the melting mood,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Drop tears as fast as the Arabian trees

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Their medicinal gum. Set you down this;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And say besides, that in Aleppo once,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Where a malignant and a turban’d Turk

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Beat a Venetian and traduced the state,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I took by the throat the circumcised dog,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And smote him, thus.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: O bloody period!

lines.speaker: GRATIANO
lines.text: All that’s spoke is marr’d.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I kiss’d thee ere I kill’d thee: no way but this;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Killing myself, to die upon a kiss.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: This did I fear, but thought he had no weapon;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: For he was great of heart.

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: To Iago. O Spartan dog,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: More fell than anguish, hunger, or the sea!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Look on the tragic loading of this bed;

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: This is thy work: the object poisons sight;

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Let it be hid. Gratiano, keep the house,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: And seize upon the fortunes of the Moor,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: For they succeed on you. To you, lord governor,

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Remains the censure of this hellish villain;

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: The time, the place, the torture: O, enforce it!

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: Myself will straight aboard: and to the state

lines.speaker: LODOVICO
lines.text: This heavy act with heavy heart relate.

stage_directions.text: What’s the matter with thee now?
Enter Montano, Gratiano, Iago, and others.
Enter Lodovico. Montano, Cassio carried in a chair, and Officers with Iago, prisoner.
Falls on the bed, and dies.

glosses.phrase: It is the cause, it is the cause, my soul
glosses.definition: the act is justified by the cause — Othello's ritual self-instruction as he approaches murder
white, translucent stone used for funeral effigies — his chosen figure for Desdemona's sleeping skin
be able to reverse my act
the divine fire Prometheus stole from the gods to animate mankind — Othello admits he cannot restore the life he is about to take
rekindle; light again
remember any sin on your conscience
make earnest petition (to heaven)
immediately
conviction; firm belief
turn my heart to stone
a sworn lie
taken arrangements

glosses.phrase: Had all his hairs been lives
glosses.definition: even if every hair on his head had been a separate life
the brief reprieve Desdemona pleads for
the moon's aberration from its course — thought to cause madness; 'lunacy' from 'luna'
wantonness; sexual sin
a precious stone, bright green-gold — see 4.1
fool
Emilia's realization of the fatal cloth's history
open and free as the north wind
without a weapon
bloody end
refrain of the willow song — Emilia dies singing it, returning the reader to 4.3
he will not die disarmed
a finely tempered Spanish blade — Toledo steel, the best in Europe
tempered (hardened) in an ice-cold stream — the superior steel-making of Spain
put an end to; obstruct
limit; boundary
the buoy marking the end of my voyage; my journey's last horizon
the great accounting; Judgment Day
damned wretch — Othello to himself
scatter me on the winds — a Dantean punishment for the damned
burn me in brimstone — hellfire
lakes of molten fire — the classical and Christian hell
half-devil — the word Othello uses for Iago

glosses.phrase: From this time forth I never will speak word
glosses.definition: Iago's final vow — his silence will outlast the play itself

glosses.phrase: loved not wisely but too well
glosses.definition: Othello's self-summary — the tragedy's most famous line of confession
Othello insists his was not the jealous temperament he displayed — contestable
the F1 reading is 'Judean' — the textual crux. 'Indian' fits a naïve native who throws away a gem; 'Judean' fits Judas casting away the pearl of salvation. Both readings are argued by editors
Desdemona; also, in the Judean reading, Christ ('the pearl of great price')
myrrh- or frankincense-producing trees — their sap, the 'medicinal gum,' was valued
eyes brought low; weeping
Syrian city under Ottoman rule
Muslim — a slur Othello applies to himself at the moment of suicide
Iago is likened to a Spartan hunting-dog, fierce and silent — Lodovico's parting epithet
the bed piled with tragic burden — the final stage picture of the play
I will embark immediately
this grievous deed

annotations.title: It is the cause: the monstrous deliberation
annotations.body:
Othello's opening soliloquy is Shakespeare's most self-conscious staging of a murder. The refrain — 'It is the cause, it is the cause, my soul' — substitutes the vocabulary of justice for the vocabulary of killing. Othello is not about to murder his wife; he is, in the language he gives himself, conducting a necessary judicial act on behalf of an abstract 'cause'.

Every image amplifies the self-deception. Desdemona is 'alabaster', a material for monuments rather than a living woman. The snuffed light becomes an emblem for a soul. Even the Promethean image — 'I know not where is that Promethean heat / That can thy light relume' — treats the body as a lamp to be refilled, not a woman to be unkilled. Shakespeare is giving us a mind that has to aestheticize its act in order to commit it. The moment her eyes open, the aesthetic will fail.
sources: Harold Bloom, *Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human* (1998)
Paul A. Kottman, *Tragic Conditions in Shakespeare* (2009)

annotations.title: Emilia's recognition and the play's closing logic
annotations.body:
Emilia's slow realization — the handkerchief, the 'eternal villain', the husband she has served without question — is the play's pivot from tragedy to reckoning. Shakespeare gives her three stages. First incredulity: 'My husband say she was false?' Second assembly: the handkerchief she gave Iago, the slander he wove. Third refusal: 'I will not charm my tongue; I am bound to speak.' Each stage takes time; the audience is made to sit through the mechanism of comprehension.

This is Shakespeare's answer to the problem of moral recognition in tragedy. Lear can see Cordelia only after the kingdom has fallen; Emilia sees Iago only after Desdemona is dead. In both cases, recognition comes too late to save but not too late to matter. Emilia's insistence that her voice will not be silenced — even by her husband's sword — is the play's one clean moral victory.
sources: Carol Thomas Neely, *Broken Nuptials in Shakespeare's Plays* (1985)

annotations.title: Indian or Judean? The famous crux
annotations.body:
Othello's final speech contains one of the most celebrated textual cruces in Shakespeare. The 1622 Quarto prints 'the base Indian, threw a pearl away / Richer than all his tribe.' The 1623 First Folio prints 'the base Judean'. Editors have argued both for four centuries.

The 'Indian' reading fits a familiar early-modern trope: the naïve native who does not recognize the value of a gem and discards it for glass. The 'Judean' reading makes the pearl Christ ('the pearl of great price' in Matthew 13:46) and the agent Judas Iscariot — or possibly Herod, who killed his wife Mariamne. Either way, 'Judean' is heavier, more theological, more culturally specific.

Arden editors (M. R. Ridley in 1958, E. A. J. Honigmann in 1997) have favored 'Judean' on intrinsic grounds; many modern productions have retained 'Indian' to preserve the orientalist valence appropriate to Othello's self-inventory. The editorial choice matters: 'Indian' makes Othello's self-condemnation colonial and worldly; 'Judean' makes it biblical and damning. Shakespeare may have revised the word. Or the compositor may have misread. Either way, the reader of the play in 2026 is reading a word chosen by an editor.
sources: M. R. Ridley (ed.), Arden 2 *Othello* (1958), note to 5.2.347
E. A. J. Honigmann (ed.), Arden 3 *Othello* (1997), Appendix 3
Richard Levin, 'The Indian/Judean Crux in *Othello*', *Shakespeare Quarterly* (1982)

annotations.title: One that loved not wisely but too well
annotations.body:
Othello's self-summary is also Shakespeare's most contested line. Is the verdict true? He was jealous — the play has shown us nothing else for two acts. He was not easily jealous — the play has shown us Iago worked on him steadily for hours. The speech labors to compose a posthumous character, exactly as the Senate speech composed a living one.

The doubleness is the point. Othello is dying, and Shakespeare is asking whether the self one dies into is the self one lived. The speech is a last piece of autobiography: the Aleppo anecdote, the turbaned Turk, the hand raised to strike. Othello dies killing the enemy of the state he served. He becomes, in his final gesture, the Venetian he spent his life being. Whether that gesture redeems the murder is a question Shakespeare leaves open.

Lodovico's verdict — 'O Spartan dog, / More fell than anguish, hunger, or the sea' — names Iago without naming Othello. The play closes with a refusal to issue a final moral judgment on its protagonist. That refusal is itself a judgment.
sources: F. R. Leavis, 'Diabolic Intellect and the Noble Hero' (1937)
Stanley Cavell, 'Othello and the Stake of the Other' (2003)
Paul A. Kottman, *Tragic Conditions in Shakespeare* (2009)

annotations.title: The tragic loading of this bed
annotations.body:
The final stage picture — three bodies on the wedding bed, Desdemona between Othello and Emilia — is the play's most concentrated image. Lodovico calls it 'the tragic loading of this bed' and orders the curtains drawn. Shakespeare gives the audience a last tableau before withdrawing it, a compositional choice that makes the final act of the tragedy the act of not-looking.

Iago, alive, is taken offstage; Cassio becomes governor of Cyprus; the state reasserts itself. The tragedy has happened in a single room, the military plot is elsewhere, and the play ends with administration. This is Shakespeare's hardest ending: the world outside the bedchamber carries on. That ordinary continuation is the tragedy's most disturbing reverberation.
sources: Philip Edwards, *Shakespeare and the Confines of Art* (1968)

trials.prompt: Othello's opening refrain 'It is the cause, it is the cause, my soul' substitutes what kind of vocabulary for the vocabulary of murder?
options: Medical terminology (as if performing surgery)
Judicial / abstract-justice language — as if conducting a necessary trial rather than killing his wife
Religious language of pilgrimage
Military command language

trials.prompt: Othello says he cannot find 'that Promethean heat that can thy light relume.' What does this admission reveal?
options: He has studied Greek mythology in depth
He acknowledges that killing Desdemona is irreversible — he cannot restore the life he is about to take
He is preparing an offering to the gods
He is doubting the existence of the Greek pantheon

trials.prompt: What finally makes Othello understand that Iago has deceived him?
options: Cassio's confession under torture
Emilia's testimony about the handkerchief — that she took it and gave it to Iago
A letter found in Roderigo's pocket after his death
A vision sent by the ghost of Brabantio

trials.prompt: Iago's final declaration — 'From this time forth I never will speak word' — is dramatically significant because:
options: It closes a subplot about his vow of chastity
The play ends with its central villain refusing to explain himself, leaving his motive forever unaccounted for
It indicates he has taken a vow of silence for religious reasons
It is a translation of a standard Italian legal formula

trials.prompt: Othello compares himself to 'the base Indian' who threw a pearl away. The Folio reads 'Judean' instead. What is at stake in this textual variant?
options: Two editors made separate typos that readers can now correct
'Indian' suggests a naïve native discarding a gem; 'Judean' suggests Judas casting away Christ — the two readings give the speech different theological weight
Both words mean exactly the same thing in Elizabethan English
The Quarto is the Shakespeare and the Folio is a forgery

trials.prompt: Othello ends his life by killing himself as 'a malignant and a turbaned Turk.' What symbolic act is he performing?
options: A ritual conversion to Islam at the moment of death
Killing the enemy of the state he once defended — dying as a Venetian by executing the Turk he has internally become
A final insult directed at the Ottoman Empire
A traditional Venetian military suicide

trials.prompt: Why does Shakespeare end the play with Cassio becoming governor and Lodovico ordering the bed curtains drawn?
options: To show that justice has been fully restored in Cyprus
To demonstrate that the world outside the bedchamber resumes ordinary governance — the tragedy reverberates precisely because life continues
Because Cassio was the original protagonist
To set up a sequel play Shakespeare never wrote
